      "title": "Summary: Negative Cash-Conversion Cycle",
      "body": "The cash conversion cycle (CCC) measures the operational and financial timeline required for a business to convert inventory and resources back into cash. This source defines the baseline calculation for the [[cash-conversion-cycle]] and details the operational mechanics of achieving a [[negative-cash-conversion-cycle]], where customer collections precede vendor payments.",
      "status": "active",

    {
      "slug": "cash-conversion-cycle",
      "page_type": "concept",
      "title": "Cash Conversion Cycle",
      "body": "The Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC) is a central financial metric tracking the number of days a company takes to convert its resource investments in inventory into cash from sales. It is mathematically calculated as:\n\n`CCC = DIO + DSO - DPO`\n\nWhere:\n- **Days Inventory Outstanding (DIO)** represents the average time required to sell inventory.\n- **Days Sales Outstanding (DSO)** measures the average time to collect customer payments.\n- **Days Payable Outstanding (DPO)** reflects the average time to pay suppliers.\n\nOptimizing these components is a primary focus of working capital management, direct liquidity, and operational efficiency. Lower metrics reduce the need for external financing.",
      "status": "active",
      "outgoing_links": [],
      "confidence": "high"
    },
    {
      "slug": "negative-cash-conversion-cycle",
      "page_type": "concept",
      "title": "Negative Cash-Conversion Cycle",
      "body": "A negative cash conversion cycle (CCC) is an operational state where a firm's Days Payable Outstanding (DPO) exceeds the sum of its Days Inventory Outstanding (DIO) and Days Sales Outstanding (DSO). Under this dynamic, a business collects cash from customer sales before its bills to suppliers are due.\n\nThis sequence effectively allows the organization to utilize its suppliers' capital as interest-free financing to fund ongoing operations and corporate growth. Large-scale retail and logistics firms like Amazon achieve a negative CCC by maintaining rapid inventory turnover, collecting fast payments from buyers, and negotiating highly extended payment terms with vendors. While advantageous for liquidity, sustaining a negative CCC requires careful management of vendor relations to avoid straining supply partners.",
      "status": "active",
      "outgoing_links": [],
      "confidence": "high"
    }
  ],
